intro:
Absalom (Hebrew Avshalom, "father of peace") was the third son of King David, born at Hebron to Maacah, daughter of the king of Geshur (2 Samuel 3:3). Famed for his flawless beauty — "from the sole of his foot to the crown of his head there was no blemish in him" — and for the heavy, luxuriant hair he cut once a year (2 Samuel 14:25–26), Absalom is one of the most tragic figures of the Old Testament: a charismatic prince whose ambition turned to treason and brought ruin on himself and grief on his father.

His story begins in violence within David's own house. When his half-brother Amnon raped Absalom's sister Tamar and David failed to act, Absalom nursed his hatred for two years and then had Amnon killed at a feast (2 Samuel 13:28–29), fleeing afterward to Geshur. Through Joab's stratagem with the wise woman of Tekoa, he was recalled to Jerusalem and at length restored to the king's presence (2 Samuel 14:33) — but reconciliation did not heal his ambition.

Absalom then mounted one of the gravest crises of David's reign. For four years he "stole the hearts of the men of Israel," rising early to stand at the gate, embracing those who came for justice and lamenting that there was no one to hear them (2 Samuel 15:6). Having won the people, he had himself proclaimed king at Hebron, and the conspiracy grew so strong that David fled Jerusalem barefoot and weeping (2 Samuel 15:10; 2 Samuel 15:30). Absalom entered the city and, on Ahithophel's counsel, publicly took his father's concubines — fulfilling Nathan's prophecy after the sin with Bathsheba that evil would rise against David from his own house (2 Samuel 16:22; cf. 2 Samuel 12:11).

The revolt ended in the forest of Ephraim, where David's forces prevailed even as the king pleaded, "Deal gently for my sake with the young man Absalom" (2 Samuel 18:5). Fleeing on his mule, Absalom's head was caught fast in the branches of a great oak, leaving him hanging between heaven and earth; there Joab, defying David's order, thrust three javelins into his heart (2 Samuel 18:9–14). When word reached the king, David's broken cry became one of Scripture's most piercing laments: "O my son Absalom, my son, my son Absalom! Would I had died instead of you, O Absalom, my son, my son!" (2 Samuel 18:33). Absalom stands as a sobering portrait of beauty and gifts corrupted by pride, of a father's grief, and of the bitter harvest of sin within a family.
